description: Adversaries send phishing messages to gain access to victim systems via spearphishing attachment link or service. Commonly uses urgency and social engineering.
Adversaries obtain and abuse credentials of existing accounts for initial access persistence privilege escalation or defense evasion.
Adversaries exploit vulnerabilities in internet-facing applications such as web servers to gain initial access to the network.
Adversaries abuse command and script interpreters such as PowerShell Bash Python or Windows Command Shell to execute commands.
Adversaries abuse task scheduling functionality to execute malicious code at system startup or on a scheduled basis for persistence.
Adversaries manipulate accounts to maintain access including modifying credentials permissions or adding SSH authorized keys.
Adversaries create new accounts on a system or domain to maintain persistent access to victim environments.
Adversaries bypass UAC sudo setuid or similar mechanisms to escalate privileges on a system.
Adversaries modify access tokens to operate under a different user or system security context via token impersonation or theft.
Adversaries exploit software vulnerabilities to escalate privileges. AssumeRole abuse and IAM policy escalation are common in cloud environments.
Adversaries delete or modify artifacts generated on a host system including logs and captured files to cover their tracks.
Adversaries disable or modify security tools such as CloudTrail GuardDuty or antivirus to avoid detection.
Adversaries use brute force techniques to attempt access when passwords are unknown including password spraying and credential stuffing.

technique: T1558 - Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets
description: Adversaries attempt to subvert Kerberos authentication by stealing or forging Kerberos tickets for lateral movement.
Adversaries steal application access tokens such as OAuth tokens AWS STS tokens to access cloud resources.
Adversaries attempt to get a listing of accounts on a system or within an environment including cloud accounts and email accounts.
Adversaries attempt to discover infrastructure within cloud environments including EC2 instances S3 buckets and IAM roles.
Adversaries log into remote services such as SSH RDP or VNC using valid accounts to move laterally within the network.
Adversaries use alternate authentication material such as stolen tokens session cookies or pass the hash to move laterally.
Adversaries access data from cloud storage such as AWS S3 buckets Azure Blobs or GCP Storage to collect sensitive information.
Adversaries steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel using the same protocol as C2 communications.
Adversaries exfiltrate data to cloud storage services or web services to avoid network-based detection.
Adversaries destroy data and files on specific systems or in large numbers on a network to interrupt availability to systems.
Adversaries encrypt data on target systems to interrupt availability. Ransomware typically encrypts files and demands payment.
